extracts.extract: Determining the questions that you are trying to answer is the most important part of a data collection process. Data should always be gathered for a reason: namely, because you think the answer to your question lies within.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Starting with research questions before collecting data is a foundational principle unaffected by AI

extracts.extract: Project design can translate seemingly fluffy principles into tangible, concrete steps and activities. It can help narrow down a possibly bewildering array of tools and techniques into those best suited for achieving responsible outcomes, and set the scene for how you and your colleagues and partners will interact with data.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Project design translating principles into concrete steps is organizational discipline independent of AI

extracts.extract: Don't design alone: think carefully about who needs to be in the room, and engage the various stakeholders or experts as soon as possible. It's usually a bad idea to bring specialists like techies or lawyers into planning processes after essential components have already been decided.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Inclusive stakeholder engagement in design is a participatory principle unaffected by technology

extracts.extract: Check yourself: give yourself opportunities for monitoring and calibrating, and don't wait for the project to be shut down before learning lessons and parsing failures. Periodically check whether you are staying true to the responsible data standards and ethical framework that you set for yourself up front.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Monitoring and calibrating throughout a project is a management principle independent of AI

extracts.extract: Document the process: track both the small and big picture approach to have a clear, organisational baseline that can be relied upon as people come and go and the project evolves. This will also make it much easier for you to draw out lessons learned from the project.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Documenting process for organizational baseline is a governance practice that applies equally to AI workflows

extracts.extract: Threat modelling is a type of risk assessment. To conduct a threat modelling exercise, identify: Assets (types of data the project will create or use), Risks (vulnerabilities in the data flow), Adversaries (individuals or groups interested in making threats real), Threats (potential harms grouped as loss, illegitimate access, or manipulation), Likelihood, and Mitigation Strategy.
extracts.chapter: Don't Panic, Plan: Assessing Risks and Threats
extracts.relevance_rationale: Threat modeling framework endures but must now include AI-specific threats like model extraction and prompt injection

extracts.extract: Feed your findings back into your project lifecycle diagram. Does this leave any critical gaps? Do you need additional resources? Should you reconsider the project in its entirety? Asking these questions will help you focus on finding the right responses.
extracts.chapter: Don't Panic, Plan: Assessing Risks and Threats
extracts.relevance_rationale: Feeding risk findings back into project design is a governance loop independent of technology

extracts.extract: Prevention is only half of what you can do. There will always be residual risk and threats you cannot foresee. This is why it is important to prepare for incidents to happen, so you can contain them or mitigate their impact. Encourage a culture of openness and learning from mistakes, not blame.
extracts.chapter: Don't Panic, Plan: Assessing Risks and Threats
extracts.relevance_rationale: Planning for incident response alongside prevention is a security principle that applies to AI systems too

extracts.extract: Which technologies do you plan to use? Work out what you need before choosing a platform: be sure it has the functionality you need to grow along with your program and that you have budgeted for development, testing, maintenance and support. No technology is ever 100% secure, but technology that has been independently audited will have fewer vulnerabilities.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Needs-first platform selection endures but must now evaluate AI-powered platforms and their data handling

extracts.extract: Trust is important, but sometimes you will need to verify that best practices are being followed. At what stages in your project do you need to assure the quality of the work being done? This can happen at the data collection stage, during data migrations and verification, or when data is analysed.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Trust-but-verify for best practices compliance is a governance principle that extends to AI tool vendors

extracts.extract: Data integrity includes aspects of both information security and information quality. Key questions: Is the data checked for changes at key points? Are changes flagged with version control? Is the data collection process well-documented through a codebook? Does the data include contextual metadata necessary to understand it?
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data integrity checks endure but must now account for AI-generated or AI-processed data quality issues

extracts.extract: Data storage mitigation strategies: only store the minimal amount of data necessary to complete the task; de-identify data by default; encrypt your data at all stages of collection, usage, transmission and storage; conduct regular audits and penetration tests of your security measures.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data minimization and de-identification are privacy principles that apply equally when AI processes data

extracts.extract: You can store some information separately from the rest. For example, if you have a database with case files, you can replace the names with codes and create a spreadsheet with the codes and their resolution to real names, then store this spreadsheet on another, highly-protected computer.
extracts.notes: Book notes this alone will not achieve true anonymisation
extracts.relevance_rationale: Separating identifiers from case data is a privacy architecture principle independent of AI

extracts.extract: A general security principle is to limit user access to only the minimum amount of data that they need to be able to do their work effectively, and only have access for as long as they need it. People should also only have the minimum permissions that they need to do their job.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Least-privilege access to data is a security principle that becomes more important with AI tools

extracts.extract: There are two main ways of getting data: using existing data (publicly available like a census, or non-released data via FOI requests, or scraping social media) and collecting new data (gathering data directly from subjects or generating it indirectly, e.g. logging GPS pings).
extracts.relevance_rationale: Two data acquisition paths still valid but now must include AI-generated synthetic data and LLM-extracted data

extracts.extract: No matter where data come from, try your best to ensure that it is good data. The most basic aspects of data quality are accuracy, validity and timeliness.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data quality fundamentals endure but LLMs can now automate accuracy, completeness, and timeliness checks

extracts.extract: Designing the data collection process requires asking: What are you collecting and why? Who provides the data and did you get their consent? Who will collect the information—ensure they are appropriate in relation to participants' identities (gender, race, class, language)? How will you collect—establish a clear process.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data collection design questions endure but must now address AI-mediated collection and consent for AI processing

extracts.extract: Consider the impact of the data collection method on desired conversational outcomes. Conversations about sensitive topics shouldn't be interrupted by the data collection tool; do what you can to remove external factors that might act as inhibitors for respondents.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Considering data collection methods impact on sensitive conversations is a human-centered design principle

extracts.extract: When deciding what data to collect, consider the minimum amount of data necessary to reach the project goals. The rationale for collection should be that it is necessary to achieve your goals, not just that it would make things easier or save you time.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data minimization principle is even more important when AI tools can easily combine and re-identify datasets

extracts.extract: Metadata can reveal a lot of information that you were deliberately not collecting, or that you want to protect. If this is the case, make purging of metadata part of the collection process from the very beginning, or configure the tools you are using to not create it in the first place.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Metadata exposure risks are amplified by AI that can extract and correlate metadata across sources at scale

extracts.extract: In some cases, paper is a good option if using electronic devices will create suspicion or risk. But also consider that electronic devices can be password protected and encrypted to protect the data in ways that paper cannot.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Choosing paper over electronic collection in hostile environments is a situational security judgment

extracts.extract: When working with existing data, remember that it was collected by someone else for specific purposes that probably don't match yours. This can lead to biases and gaps. Try to gain insight into the context before using it: Who published/licensed the data? Who funded the process? What biases might it hold? What methods were used to collect it?
extracts.relevance_rationale: Skepticism toward inherited data collected for other purposes is an epistemological principle that applies to AI training data too

extracts.extract: Data should be usable as well as useful. Data will need to be machine readable for most types of analysis. Moving data from one format to another needs to be planned and executed carefully, so as not to lose information or inadvertently manipulate the data. Make sure to keep versions, and clearly document your steps.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data format conversion still essential but LLMs can now automate many format transformations

extracts.extract: Even when data is de-identified, anonymized or aggregated so individuals are not immediately apparent, the possibility to re-identify individuals is always there, especially when combining that data with complementary sources. This is the Mosaic effect.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Re-identification risk amplified by LLMs ability to cross-reference and infer identities from sparse data

extracts.extract: It's worth spending time trying to identify types of people reflected by data, even when they are not readily apparent. Organize this as a brainstorming exercise with the project team, to try and work out as much as possible about the people behind a found data set, then use those intuitions to consider relevant responsibilities.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Brainstorming hidden populations in data is a human ethical exercise unchanged by AI

extracts.extract: When presenting existing data, it is critical to include information about the data's provenance and the context in which it was produced. Being explicit about the data's contextual unknowns is essential for enabling others to manage your project's data responsibly.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Presenting data provenance and contextual unknowns is a core transparency principle unaffected by AI

extracts.extract: Informed consent is composed of three components: disclosure of research objectives and any risks of participating; capacity of individuals to understand implications of participating; and voluntariness of their participation.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Informed consent components (disclosure, capacity, voluntariness) are foundational ethical principles

extracts.extract: Consent should be linked to a clearly identifiable purpose rather than a vague, omnibus, open-ended one. Disclosure should include: the nature and objectives of the project, the purpose for which data is collected, how the data will be used, what risks the data and its use might pose, and what opportunities data subjects will have to review and revoke consent.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Purpose-linked consent and disclosure checklists are enduring data ethics requirements

extracts.extract: Research increasingly indicates that consent has its limitations. Due to bounded rationality, people cannot possibly understand what they are consenting to and what possible uses their data might be put to in the future. Projects should be wary of relying wholly on consent to legitimize any sort of practice or wide use of data.
extracts.notes: Citing Daniel Solove on privacy self-management
extracts.relevance_rationale: Consent limitations even more acute when LLMs can repurpose data in ways individuals cannot foresee

extracts.extract: Examine the raw data before doing anything to manipulate it. This includes making a complete backup of the original material, kept separate from any other activities. This can be vital for ensuring accuracy, for availability in case of accidental deletion, and for responding to accusations of manipulation, for example in a court situation.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Backing up raw data before manipulation is fundamental data integrity practice

extracts.extract: Go back and ask: reach out to a small sample of the surveyed population for more in-depth analysis. If the initial group was part of a small qualitative research project, you might scale up through a more quantitative questionnaire to support your first research.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Re-validation through sampling and follow-up is a methodological principle independent of tools

extracts.extract: Cross-checking: if there are other data available about the same subject, look at how they compare. With video footage, this can include examining extra metadata (time, location, camera type), matching location features with satellite photos or maps, and checking whether activity is validated by other information sources like reports, social media, or newspapers.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Cross-checking and triangulation are core verification principles that endure regardless of AI

extracts.extract: Messy data may hide harmful information. If we don't make sure that we can clearly name, describe and recognise all the information contained in data sets, we might make improper assumptions about the risks that data might pose. Gaps and assumptions come to the fore much more clearly after creating some order.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Recognizing messy data may hide harmful information is an ethical imperative unchanged by AI

extracts.extract: Cleaning data can mean: finding and removing unwanted bits of data in spreadsheets, formatting data correctly for the tools you are using, dealing with inconsistencies in the data, and structuring it so it can be used effectively.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core cleaning tasks endure but LLMs can now automate much of the formatting and consistency work

extracts.extract: Filtering is usually the first step in checking data for consistency. It creates a menu-like list of data aggregates present in a column. Tools like Microsoft Excel, Libre Office Calc, Apple Numbers or Google Sheets all offer strong filtering functionalities. A first filtering round will already offer an overview of how comparable the data set is and where the main need for cleanup lies.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Spreadsheet filtering still useful but LLMs offer conversational data exploration as alternative

extracts.extract: For more advanced data cleanup, Open Refine offers powerful ways to combine, compare and reconcile your data. Open Refine lets you combine and aggregate slightly differing types of data using algorithms that assist with fuzzy comparisons of not-quite-equal information. It also lets you execute faceted search on your data.
extracts.relevance_rationale: OpenRefine fuzzy matching endures but LLMs now offer powerful entity resolution and reconciliation

extracts.extract: A complementary aspect of data cleanup is making sure the information that has been collected is fully and exhaustively described. Describing information means documenting and collecting metadata (data about data). When preparing the data set for analysis, look into all the metadata you might have and make sure they are documented.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Documenting and collecting metadata is essential data stewardship regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: It is smart to adopt common standards and file formats for the data, so that they can be more easily shareable, more resilient (future-proof) and also comparable with other data sets—i.e. interoperable. One example is the CSV format. Having data live in a proprietary format like .xls might mean that one day the company that owns it stops supporting it, the data set becomes unusable, and the knowledge is lost.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Adopting open standards and interoperable formats is future-proofing wisdom independent of AI

extracts.extract: All data, no matter how it is collected, will contain a certain amount of bias. It is your task to analyse what those biases might be, to minimise them to the extent possible, identify the ones that cannot be removed, and make sure that persistent biases are well known and explicitly flagged throughout the research.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Acknowledging bias in all data and working to minimize it is a foundational epistemological principle

extracts.extract: Bias is the tendency of results to favor a certain outcome, due to the implicit construction or logic of the collection or processing of the data, the way that the data was collected (setting, sequence of questions) and the way that the data is analysed. Being aware of sampling bias, how it impacts analysis and limits what we can say about the overall population is important.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Understanding sampling bias, methodology limits, and bias typology is core analytical literacy

extracts.extract: Take special care if you are working with comparative data: differences in collection techniques, or even differing definitions, might seriously skew your results. If you are collecting and comparing data on sexual abuse between several countries, terms like assault and rape might have very different definitions in different places.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Definitional bias in comparative data is a methodological challenge unchanged by AI

extracts.extract: Consider testing for data collection bias in your results. Often results may be accidentally or deliberately manipulated by the people collecting the data. Participants may fear losing benefits if they give negative answers. The collector may have had trouble gaining access to the correct mix of participants. Check for this by re-validating a small sample.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Testing for collection bias and social desirability bias is enduring methodological practice

extracts.extract: Every data set contains outliers—data points that are so different from all others that they really skew the results. These can be anomalies or may just be errors in data entry. Going through the data set carefully and removing these is part of the data analyst's standard toolkit. However, make sure you are only reducing data noise, not changing the data to fit your expected outcome.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Identifying outliers and guarding against confirmation bias are core analytical skills

extracts.extract: Even if two variables might seem to be related, it doesn't mean that one caused the other. The classic example is the correlation between crime rates and ice cream consumption during summer months. The two variables are correlated, but neither causes the other—both are causally linked to temperature.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Correlation vs causation is foundational statistical literacy that persists regardless of tools

extracts.extract: Connect with experts in the field for opinion: experts working in the field you are doing research on might provide a valuable resource in knowing what your results actually mean, and whether there are gaps or blind spots in your research that you should address before starting the analysis.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Seeking domain expert review to catch blind spots is essential human judgment practice

extracts.extract: Once data is collected, it can be released at various levels: closed networks within an organisation, platform-dependent sharing between peer organisations, publishing with closed licenses, or publishing with fully open licenses. Carefully consider the various forms that sharing can take.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data sharing spectrum from closed to open is a governance framework independent of AI

extracts.extract: Be aware of the blind spots that internal sharing often entails: you are probably seeking feedback from like-minded individuals who share your own biases and won't provide truly independent feedback. The wider you go, the more diverse feedback will be, but the less control you have.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Echo chamber awareness in internal feedback is an organizational insight unchanged by AI

extracts.extract: Data aggregation is a crucial point of risk assessment: sometimes standalone data deemed safe becomes harmful when combined with other data sets, or data that you thought was anonymised becomes easily discernible once combined with other data, using triangulation techniques.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data aggregation re-identification risk intensified by LLMs ability to combine and infer across datasets

extracts.extract: Sharing your data with unusual suspects will provide you with opinions outside the echo chamber you are used to. You could share the data back with the people reflected in the data and see what they think of what it reveals.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Seeking feedback from unusual suspects outside echo chambers is enduring organizational practice

extracts.extract: Once the data set is shared publicly, the proverbial cat is out of the bag. Any personally identifiable information that hasn't been properly addressed will be impossible to mitigate because someone might have already made a copy. A final risk assessment of the data is more than warranted before publication.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Irreversibility of public data release and PII risks are permanent concerns regardless of AI

extracts.extract: There might be no such thing as perfect anonymisation. De-identified data sets can be combined with other supposedly anonymous data to re-identify individuals. This chapter uses the term de-identification rather than anonymisation because it doesn't have the magic bullet feeling and because the word itself implies that it can be undone.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Anonymisation limits more acute now that LLMs can re-identify from minimal data points via inference

extracts.extract: Anonymisation techniques: Data masking (supplying only part of a data set or deleting columns), Pseudonymisation (exchanging values for codes), Aggregation (rounding large numbers or providing only small samples), Derived data (providing the age of a person instead of the exact date of birth).
extracts.relevance_rationale: Masking, pseudonymisation, aggregation, and derived data are enduring anonymisation techniques

extracts.extract: Create an expert group of advisors who have experience with your project and with releasing data safely. Ideally some will be outsiders without biased connections to the project. Also seek advice from people with skills in de-anonymising, such as data scientists, open data experts and ethical hackers.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Expert advisory and adversarial red-teaming before data release is enduring governance practice

extracts.extract: No data is neutral, and presenting information can reveal even more biases. Use data as accurately as possible, and take care not to misrepresent topics or skew the data in order to suit your particular advocacy or campaigning need—it will make for an unreliable and easily discreditable campaign.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data is never neutral; accurate presentation without advocacy skew is core editorial ethics

extracts.extract: When presenting data, ensure that marginalised groups are appropriately treated and they are not being omitted, trivialised, judged or romanticised. Be intentional in how they are represented and what this means for your participants and beyond.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Responsible representation of marginalised groups in data presentation is enduring ethical principle

extracts.extract: Data can never represent all people and all nuances of a cultural or social mosaic. It is vital to remember that data is inherently exclusive and rarely tells the whole story. When collecting or finding data, ask yourself: who does the data leave out? Who is not reflected and what impact might that have?
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data being inherently exclusive and never telling the whole story is fundamental epistemology

extracts.extract: Transparency calls for disclosure: keeping an open and easily accessible account of methodology, intent of data use, who will use it and for how long, as well as how the data, the analyses and the learnings will be shared. Privacy should be granted to those without power; transparency should be demanded of those with power.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Transparency in methodology disclosure and power asymmetry awareness are enduring principles

extracts.extract: If you intend on using your data in legal cases, data management systems must: allow for an auditable chain of custody, ensure the integrity and authenticity of the data (e.g. through hashing and forensic examination), and ensure that data content itself is verifiable.
extracts.chapter: What Types of Laws and Procedures Apply to Your Data Project?
extracts.relevance_rationale: Chain of custody, hashing, and forensic integrity for legal evidence are enduring requirements

extracts.extract: Legal systems often include a purpose limitation principle, in which data collected for one purpose cannot be used for any other purpose without the consent of the data subject. Data sharing can challenge this foundational principle, especially if the common principle of data minimisation was also ignored.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Purpose limitation and data minimisation are foundational legal principles unchanged by AI

extracts.extract: Published data or online data is not necessarily open data. Data published as an Excel table within a PDF document, without an open license, is not open data because it can't be easily managed or re-used. Whether or not you want to open your data is an important consideration.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Distinguishing open data from merely published data is an enduring definitional insight

extracts.extract: If you don't license your data properly, others won't know if or how they can use it. Licensing makes it clear how you want your data to be used. Retaining attribution makes it easier to see how your data has been used by other parties and to maintain some level of accountability to the data owners.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Proper licensing and attribution for data accountability are enduring governance practices

extracts.extract: Deciding what to do with the data after it has served its purpose is an integral part of the project design process and should be informed by the risk assessment. Map where the data is, ensure responsible data procedures are upheld after closing, and dispose of or archive appropriately.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Planning data disposal at project design stage is enduring lifecycle management practice

extracts.extract: A good principle from the information security community is to only have the data you need for the time when you need it. If it is important to keep the data forever, you must plan for resources to continually update and support safe data management practices for the data.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Only keeping data you need for the time you need it is enduring data minimisation principle

extracts.extract: Try to store data to be archived in popular, interoperable formats which are likely to be used in future. Over the course of a few years formats may go out of use and software systems become unable to use the data.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Storing archives in popular interoperable formats for future-proofing is enduring practice

extracts.extract: Collecting data about someone creates an inherent power imbalance to the extent that the data collector effectively owns a commodity relating intimately to an individual. That data could have negative consequences for the data subject, such as whether or not they receive vital services.
extracts.chapter: The World of Data: Opportunities and Risks
extracts.relevance_rationale: Power imbalance inherent in data collection is a foundational ethical insight unchanged by AI

extracts.extract: Adopting a critical approach to avert a data for data's sake methodology will go a long way towards ensuring fairness and balance. Future-proofing is also an issue: what seems unproblematic data right now may turn out to be very sensitive in the future. Minimizing the data collected can in itself be a responsible measure.
extracts.chapter: The World of Data: Opportunities and Risks
extracts.relevance_rationale: Critical approach against data-for-datas-sake and future-proofing concerns are enduring principles

extracts.extract: Removing all personally identifiable information from a data set isn't necessarily enough to protect identities. With increasing sophistication of analytical techniques and algorithms, de-identified data sets can be combined with other supposedly anonymous data to re-identify individuals. This phenomenon, known as the Mosaic Effect, is particularly challenging because evaluating the risk requires anticipating all data sets that might be combined with yours.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Re-identification via algorithmic analysis is now dramatically easier with LLMs cross-referencing capability

extracts.extract: The first step for live data processes is to clearly define who should have access to exactly what data at which point. Create a Roles-Responsibilities-Functionality Map to identify who should do what and when.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Role-based access control and workflow mapping for live data are enduring organizational practices

extracts.extract: For sharing data with a limited set of actors, consider whether data sharing should be governed by explicit agreements such as MoUs or contracts. Entering into agreements about the conditions, limitations and ethical guidelines can impose some measure of control and establish shared expectations.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data sharing agreements and governance through MoUs/contracts are enduring organizational practices

extracts.extract: Managing data responsibly is not simply the task of one person: it's an overarching theme to be considered throughout (and after\!) the lifecycle of a project. Ensuring appropriate and responsible processes will require the input and engagement of researchers, communications focal points, technical staff, enumerators and local consultants.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Cross-functional team responsibility for data lifecycle is enduring organizational principle

extracts.extract: How you store your data should be measured against the particular risks you or your partners may be facing, and your personal priorities: are you more concerned about data loss, or surveillance? No method of storing data is 100% safe, so it is essential that you backup your data, no matter how or where it is stored.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Risk-based storage decisions balancing data loss vs surveillance are enduring security considerations

extracts.extract: Deleting digital information is more than just clicking delete. As a general rule, everything that is on a hard drive needs to be overwritten several times. There are free tools to help you do this.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Secure deletion principles endure but specific overwriting techniques need updating for cloud and AI-era storage
